primary_disability__health_background.primary_disability_health_background_des:
Primary disability: Incomplete spinal cord injury at T11 level resulting from a motor vehicle accident in November 2019. This has led to significant lower-limb weakness, reduced balance, chronic neuropathic pain, and reduced endurance, which together affect my ability to safely complete day-to-day activities at home and in the community.

Mobility and physical function:
- I primarily mobilise with a manual wheelchair for longer distances (shopping centres, appointments, and outdoor activities). Around the home I use a four-wheeled walker for short distances (e.g., bedroom to bathroom, kitchen to lounge), but I fatigue quickly and am at risk of losing balance, especially when carrying items or turning.
- I experience increased spasticity in my legs in the evenings, which can make transfers from wheelchair to bed or toilet slower and more effortful. I have had two minor falls in the last 12 months when transferring from the couch to the walker, luckily without serious injury, but they have affected my confidence.
- I can manage basic transfers independently most days (bed, wheelchair, toilet, shower chair) but need grab rails and a stable surface. On days when my pain is worse, my partner often needs to stand by to supervise or provide light physical assistance.

Pain and fatigue:
- I have chronic neuropathic pain in my lower back, hips, and legs, rated typically 5–7/10, worse after prolonged sitting or when the weather is cold. This pain makes it hard to sit in one position for more than 45–60 minutes.
- Fatigue is a significant issue. If I have a busy morning (e.g., medical appointments, errands), I often need to rest for 1–2 hours in the afternoon, which limits my ability to participate in work-like activities or social events consistently.
- I am currently on medication prescribed by my GP and pain specialist (including a low-dose neuropathic pain medication and anti-inflammatories as needed). These help somewhat but can cause drowsiness and difficulty concentrating.

Daily living activities (ADLs and IADLs):
- Personal care: I can shower using a shower chair and handheld shower but require grab rails and non-slip mats. I can wash my upper body independently. I sometimes need help from my partner to wash my lower legs and feet when my back is particularly stiff or when I am having more pain.
- Dressing: I can dress my upper body independently. Lower body dressing takes significantly longer (around 15–20 minutes) and I sometimes need my partner to assist with socks and shoes, particularly on high-pain days.
- Toileting: I use a raised toilet seat and grab rails. I am independent most of the time but have had a couple of near-falls when transferring quickly at night.
- Meal preparation: I can prepare simple meals (e.g., sandwiches, reheating pre-prepared meals, basic cooking) if I can sit on a perching stool at the bench. Standing for more than 10–15 minutes at a time causes increased pain and fatigue. I struggle with heavier tasks like lifting large pots, carrying groceries from the car, or bending to access low cupboards and the oven.
- Housework: I can manage light tasks such as wiping benches and stacking the dishwasher, but I have difficulty with vacuuming, mopping, changing bed linen, and carrying laundry baskets. My partner currently completes most heavier household chores, which puts pressure on them as they also work full-time.

Home and environment:
- We live in a single-storey rented house in Preston (VIC) with three steps at the front entrance and a narrow hallway leading to the bathroom. The bathroom has a standard shower-over-bath configuration, which is not ideal, and requires careful transfers to a shower chair. There are no built-in grab rails in the bathroom or toilet—only temporary suction rails that feel unstable.
- The front steps and narrow doorway make it difficult for me to independently leave the house in my wheelchair. At present, I rely on my partner to assist me in getting the wheelchair in and out of the house, which limits my independence and flexibility (e.g., I cannot easily go out alone during the day).

Psychosocial background and participation:
- Prior to the accident I worked full-time as an electrician and was very physically active (played social soccer twice a week and went hiking). I have not been able to return to my previous job due to physical demands and safety concerns.
- I am currently exploring part-time, less physically demanding work or study options, but my fatigue, pain, and limited endurance make it difficult to commit to regular hours.
- I sometimes feel low in mood and frustrated about my loss of independence, especially when I have to rely on my partner for basic tasks like getting in and out of the house or managing heavier chores. I am linked with a psychologist through my GP, which helps, but I feel that improved functional capacity and better equipment would significantly increase my confidence and participation.

Reason for referral and goals for OT/physio services:
- I am seeking a comprehensive functional capacity assessment to clearly document my current abilities and support needs across self-care, mobility, domestic tasks, and community participation for my NDIS plan review.
- I would like assessment and recommendations for home modifications (especially safe access in and out of the house, and improved bathroom/toilet safety) so that I can move around more independently and reduce my fall risk.
- I am interested in life skills training and graded activity planning to help me manage fatigue, build endurance, and safely take on more household responsibilities and possibly return to some form of work or training.
- I would also like input into pain management strategies (including positioning, pacing, and gentle strengthening/physiotherapy programs) to reduce the impact of pain on my daily activities and sleep.

Other relevant health information:
- Past medical history: Incomplete T11 spinal cord injury (2019), mild hypertension (managed with medication), and a history of anxiety/depressive symptoms following the accident.
- Medications: Neuropathic pain medication, anti-inflammatory as needed, and an antihypertensive. All current medications are prescribed and monitored by my GP and pain specialist.
- Supports: I live with my partner, who provides informal support with heavier household tasks and supervision during higher-risk transfers. I also have a small circle of close friends and family who assist with transport and social support when needed.

Overall, my main aims are to improve safety and independence at home, reduce strain on my partner, and increase my capacity to engage in meaningful activities such as community access, social participation, and a gradual return to work or study.
